roman_empire.name: Roman Empire
roman_empire.summary:
At the dawn of the Common Era, the Roman Empire stands at the height of its power, stretching from the windswept shores of Britannia to the sun-baked deserts of Mesopotamia. Under Augustus Caesar, Rome has transformed from a turbulent republic into a stable autocracy—the Principate—while maintaining the outward forms of republican governance. The Mediterranean Sea, which Romans call mare nostrum ("our sea"), is ringed entirely by Roman territory, making it the world's most impressive political achievement to date.

The Pax Romana—the Roman Peace—has begun. For the next two centuries, trade will flourish, cities will grow, and the roads radiating from Rome will carry goods, soldiers, ideas, and diseases to every corner of the known Western world. Legions patrol frontiers on the Rhine and Danube, holding back Germanic tribes, while in the East, a tense equilibrium with the Parthian Empire defines the edge of Roman ambition.
key_events: Augustus establishes the Principate — the first Roman Emperor
Pax Romana begins, bringing relative peace for 200 years
Roman legions suffer catastrophic defeat at Battle of Teutoburg Forest (9 AD)
Silk Road trade connects Rome commercially to Parthia, India, and Han China
roman_empire.did_you_know: The Roman road network stretched over 400,000 km — enough to circle the Earth ten times. Engineers used a standard road-building technique with gravel layers and drainage ditches that remained unsurpassed in Europe for 1,500 years.

han.name: Han Dynasty
han.summary:
The Han Dynasty rules over an empire that rivals Rome in size and sophistication, governing a population that may exceed Rome's. From its capital at Luoyang, the Han Emperor commands a vast bureaucracy selected through one of history's earliest civil service examinations—a meritocratic innovation that will define Chinese governance for millennia.

Han China controls the eastern terminus of the Silk Road, exporting silk, porcelain, and paper westward in exchange for glass, gold, and horses. The Great Wall, recently extended and garrisoned, guards the northern frontier against Xiongnu raiders. Han armies have pushed deep into Central Asia, and Chinese influence reaches as far as Korea and Vietnam.
han.ruler: Emperor Ping of Han (r. 1 BC – 6 AD)
key_events: Han Confucianism becomes the state ideology and basis for civil examinations
Ban Chao extends Han influence into Central Asia and contacts Parthia
Zhang Heng invents the seismometer to detect earthquakes
Paper is invented during this era, transforming record-keeping
han.did_you_know: Han China and Rome were contemporaries and knew of each other vaguely through Parthian middlemen — the Chinese called Rome 'Daqin.' Direct diplomatic contact was rarely achieved, and neither empire fully understood the other's scale.

parthian_empire.name: Parthian Empire
parthian_empire.summary:
Sandwiched between Rome to the west and Han China to the east, the Parthian Empire occupies one of history's most strategically vital positions. Controlling the central Silk Road, Parthian merchants and nobles profit enormously from trade between the great civilizations at either end of Eurasia.

The Parthians are master horsemen and archers. Their heavy cavalry—the cataphract—is among the most fearsome military force of the ancient world. Rome has learned this painfully: at Carrhae (53 BC), the Parthians destroyed a Roman army under Crassus, killing 20,000 Romans and capturing 10,000 more. The Roman-Parthian border has simmered with tension ever since, with Armenia as the perennial flashpoint.
key_events: Parthia acts as the crucial middleman in Silk Road trade between Rome and China
Battle of Carrhae (53 BC) establishes Parthia's reputation against Roman legions
Armenia contested between Rome and Parthia — a diplomatic and military flashpoint
Parthian architecture and art blend Greek and Persian traditions
parthian_empire.did_you_know: The Parthians were famed for the 'Parthian shot' — the technique of firing arrows backwards while retreating on horseback, a tactic so effective it became proverbial and gave us the phrase 'parting shot.'

satavahanihara.name: Satavahana Kingdom
satavahanihara.summary:
The Satavahana dynasty rules over a prosperous kingdom in the Deccan plateau of central India, controlling the overland and coastal trade routes that connect the Roman world to Southeast Asia and China. Satavahana ports on both coasts bustle with Roman, Arab, and Southeast Asian merchants exchanging spices, textiles, and precious stones.

This era sees the flourishing of early Buddhism and the construction of magnificent rock-cut cave temples at sites like Ajanta and Ellora. The Satavahana kings patronize both Brahmanical and Buddhist traditions, reflecting the religious pluralism that characterizes Indian civilization.
key_events: Indian Ocean trade with Rome reaches its peak — pepper, cotton, spices flow westward
Buddhist cave temples commissioned at Karla and Bhaja
Periplus of the Erythraean Sea documents Indian trade routes for Roman merchants
Sanskrit literature flourishes under Satavahana patronage
satavahanihara.did_you_know: Roman archaeologists have found hoards of Roman gold coins across southern India, testament to how intensely Romans craved Indian spices and textiles — the Roman Senate repeatedly complained that gold was draining from the empire to pay for Indian luxuries.

axum.name: Kingdom of Aksum
axum.summary:
Rising in the highlands of what is now Ethiopia, the Kingdom of Aksum is one of the world's great trading powers, controlling the Red Sea trade that connects the Mediterranean to Arabia, India, and East Africa. Aksumite merchants dominate the lucrative ivory, gold, and incense trade, and the kingdom's currency—gold, silver, and bronze coins—circulates across the ancient world.

Aksum sits at the crossroads of civilizations, absorbing influences from South Arabia, Egypt, Greece, and India while developing its own distinctive monumental architecture: the towering stelae (obelisks) that mark royal tombs and proclaim Aksumite power to the world.
axum.ruler: Unknown king (Aksum's early rulers are poorly documented)
key_events: Aksum controls Red Sea trade between Roman Egypt and Arabia/India
Construction of enormous granite stelae — some reaching 33 meters tall
Gold coinage minted, showing Aksum's wealth and sophistication
Cultural exchange with South Arabia, Greece, and Egypt
axum.did_you_know: The Aksumites would later, in the 4th century AD, become one of the first states in the world to officially adopt Christianity as a state religion — decades before the Roman Empire did so.

scythians.name: Scythian / Sarmatian Tribes
scythians.summary:
Across the vast steppes stretching from the Danube to the Altai Mountains, nomadic peoples — the Scythians and their successors the Sarmatians — maintain a way of life built entirely around the horse. These skilled horsemen trade with both Rome and the civilizations to their east, raiding when opportunity arises and trading when it suits them.

By 1 AD, the Scythians proper are fading, replaced by the Sarmatians who have pushed westward. These are warrior aristocracies whose noblewomen sometimes fight alongside men — a reality that may have inspired Greek legends of the Amazons. Their burial mounds (kurgans) dot the Eurasian steppe, filled with gold, weapons, and horses for the afterlife.
key_events: Sarmatians displace earlier Scythian tribes and push toward the Roman Danube frontier
Steppe nomads serve as mercenaries for both Rome and Parthia
Rich kurgan burial culture produces extraordinary goldwork
Nomadic trade networks connect Eastern Europe to Central Asia
scythians.did_you_know: Scythian and Sarmatian women were not merely mythological warriors — archaeology has confirmed that a significant percentage of women buried in warrior graves were indeed equipped with weapons and showed combat injuries, inspiring the Amazon legends.

maya_chiefdoms_and_states.name: Mayan Kingdoms
maya_chiefdoms_and_states.summary:
In the tropical lowlands and highlands of Mesoamerica, dozens of independent Mayan city-states are entering what scholars call the Late Preclassic period — the foundation for the extraordinary Classic Maya civilization that will peak around 250-900 AD. Cities like El Mirador, already one of the largest pre-Columbian cities ever built, demonstrate the Mayas' remarkable architectural and organizational capabilities.

The Maya have developed one of history's most sophisticated writing systems, an accurate astronomical calendar, and a mathematical concept of zero — intellectual achievements that will remain unmatched in the Americas for centuries. Their kings rule as divine intermediaries between humans and the cosmos.
key_events: El Mirador reaches its peak — possibly the largest Maya city ever built
Long Count calendar and hieroglyphic writing system fully developed
Monumental temple construction across the Yucatan and Guatemala
Trade networks connecting Maya cities with Teotihuacan in Central Mexico
maya_chiefdoms_and_states.did_you_know: The Maya independently developed the concept of zero and positional notation for numbers — at roughly the same time as mathematicians in India were doing the same, with no known contact between them. Zero is one of history's most powerful mathematical ideas.
